dependencies.description: Requires IP reachability between neighbors (multicast or unicast for NBMA)
dependencies.name: IP connectivity

dependencies.description: Requires multicast support for 224.0.0.5/224.0.0.6 on broadcast/P2P networks
dependencies.name: IP multicast

dependencies.description: Hello/dead intervals, area ID, authentication, and network type must match between neighbors
dependencies.name: Matching OSPF parameters

dependencies.description: OSPF process must be configured with network statements or interface-level 'ip ospf' commands
dependencies.name: OSPF process and network statements

dependencies.description: OSPF uses raw IP (protocol 89), not TCP or UDP. Hellos are multicast to 224.0.0.5 (AllSPFRouters) on broadcast and point-to-point networks, and DR/BDR receive updates via 224.0.0.6 (AllDRouters). In SONiC the interface must have multicast enabled and any ACLs or CoPP policies must permit IP protocol 89.
dependencies.name: IP Protocol 89 multicast to 224.0.0.5 and 224.0.0.6

dependencies.description: OSPF in SONiC runs inside the 'bgp' Docker container via FRR. ospfd handles the OSPF FSM, LSA flooding, and SPF computation. zebra receives computed OSPF routes from ospfd via the FRR inter-process socket and installs them into the Linux kernel RIB. Both must be running for OSPF to function.
dependencies.name: FRR stack (ospfd + zebra)

dependencies.description: Translates CONFIG_DB OSPF entries into FRR vtysh commands. Required for CONFIG_DB-driven OSPF configuration to reach FRR. Runs inside the bgp container. Note: not all SONiC builds have full OSPF CONFIG_DB support -- some deployments configure OSPF directly via vtysh or frr.conf.
dependencies.name: frrcfgd

dependencies.description: Subscribes to Linux kernel netlink events for route changes (including OSPF routes installed by zebra) and publishes them to APPL_DB ROUTE_TABLE. Without fpmsyncd, OSPF routes in the kernel RIB never reach orchagent or the ASIC. Runs inside the bgp container alongside ospfd and zebra.
dependencies.name: fpmsyncd

dependencies.description: Consumes APPL_DB ROUTE_TABLE entries (regardless of source protocol) and translates them into SAI API calls written to ASIC_DB. OSPF routes are not programmed into hardware until orchagent processes them successfully.
dependencies.name: orchagent (swss container)

dependencies.description: Reads ASIC_DB and calls the vendor SAI library to program the physical ASIC forwarding tables. The final step in the OSPF route programming pipeline.
dependencies.name: syncd (syncd container)

dependencies.description: Source of truth for OSPF configuration in SONiC deployments that use frrcfgd for OSPF config management. Not all SONiC builds have full OSPF CONFIG_DB table support -- check for DEVICE_METADATA and OSPF-specific tables.
dependencies.name: CONFIG_DB (Redis DB 4)

dependencies.description: Route publication bus between fpmsyncd and orchagent. OSPF routes must appear in ROUTE_TABLE here before they can reach the ASIC.
dependencies.name: APPL_DB (Redis DB 0)

dependencies.description: Bidirectional Forwarding Detection provides sub-second neighbor failure detection for OSPF. When BFD declares a neighbor down, ospfd tears down the adjacency faster than the Dead Interval. Configured in FRR as 'ip ospf bfd' on the interface.
dependencies.name: BFD (optional)

failure_modes.description: When OSPF and another IGP (typically EIGRP) are mutually redistributed at two or more boundary routers, routes can be learned back from the wrong protocol with a better administrative distance or metric, creating routing loops or black holes. Routes redistributed from OSPF into EIGRP at Router A may be redistributed back into OSPF at Router B with a different metric, potentially overriding the original OSPF route.

resolutions.action: Use route tags: tag routes during redistribution into OSPF ('set tag <N>'), then filter on re-entry: 'deny tag <N>' in the redistribution from OSPF back to the other IGP

Adjust administrative distance: make native OSPF routes (AD 110) preferred over OSPF external routes redistributed from another protocol
Adjust administrative distance: make native OSPF routes (AD 110) preferred over OSPF external routes redistributed from another protocol
Apply prefix-lists at redistribution points: only redistribute specific prefixes that belong in each domain
Apply prefix-lists at redistribution points: only redistribute specific prefixes that belong in each domain
Apply prefix-lists at redistribution points: only redistribute specific prefixes that belong in each domain
Consider one-way redistribution where possible — redistribute INTO OSPF but use default route for return traffic
Consider one-way redistribution where possible — redistribute INTO OSPF but use default route for return traffic
Consider one-way redistribution where possible — redistribute INTO OSPF but use default route for return traffic
Reduce redistribution points: if possible, redistribute at only one boundary router and use the other as backup with higher metrics

Reduce redistribution points: if possible, redistribute at only one boundary router and use the other as backup with higher metrics

root_causes.cause: Mutual redistribution at two or more boundary routers without loop prevention
No route tags configured to identify and filter redistributed routes
Administrative distance not adjusted — redistributed routes may have better AD than native routes at certain points in the topology
Missing prefix-list or route-map at redistribution points to prevent routes from being redistributed back into their source protocol
Seed metric mismatch causing the redistributed path to appear better than the native path

failure_modes.description: Traffic takes different forward and return paths through the network due to ECMP, unequal IGP costs, or multi-exit routing. While asymmetric routing is not inherently a routing problem, it breaks stateful inspection devices (firewalls, NAT, IPS, load balancers) that expect to see both directions of a flow. The firewall drops return traffic because it never saw the initial SYN, causing application failures.

root_causes.cause: ECMP load balancing across multiple firewalls — forward and return flows hashed to different devices
Dual-homed site with two ISP connections — outbound via ISP-A, inbound via ISP-B
Multiple routing protocol paths with different metrics in each direction
Hot Standby/VRRP failover with routing not aligned — FHRP active on one router but routing prefers a different exit
Policy-based routing (PBR) applied only in one direction

failure_modes.description: Two OSPF routers on the same segment have different Hello Interval or Dead Interval values configured. When Hello packets are received, the intervals in the received Hello are compared against the local interface configuration. A mismatch causes the Hello to be silently ignored -- no error message is generated, no adjacency forms, and the neighbor never advances past Down state. This is one of the most common OSPF misconfiguration errors and is especially subtle because ospfd logs do not always surface the mismatch reason.

resolutions.action: Capture Hello packets on both sides: 'sudo tcpdump -i <interface> proto ospf -c 20' and compare Hello Interval and Dead Interval fields
Check local OSPF interface config: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf interface <ifname>"' -- shows configured hello-interval and dead-interval
Check peer's OSPF interface config via out-of-band or console access
Align Hello and Dead intervals: 'ip ospf hello-interval <N>' and 'ip ospf dead-interval <N>' on both interfaces in vtysh (or via frrcfgd/config)
Verify change took effect: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf interface <ifname>"'

root_causes.cause: Hello Interval configured differently on the two ends of the link
Dead Interval configured differently on the two ends of the link
One side uses 'ip ospf dead-interval minimal hello-multiplier' fast timers and the other uses standard timers
Configuration applied to wrong interface or missing on one side
frrcfgd rendered different timer values from CONFIG_DB due to template issue

failure_modes.scenario: Hello/Dead interval mismatch -- adjacency never forms
symptoms: 'show ip ospf neighbor' shows no neighbor entry for the expected peer IP
Neighbor is permanently in Down state or never appears
Interface shows ospf enabled but neighbor count is 0
tcpdump on the interface shows OSPF Hello packets arriving from the peer
ospfd logs may show 'Packet[Hello:RECV]: NetworkMask mismatch' or interval mismatch message

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf interface <ifname>'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Different Hello or Dead Interval values between the two ends confirms the mismatch
verify_commands.look_for: Timer intervals configured: Hello <N>s, Dead <N>s -- compare with expected peer values

verify_commands.confirms_if: Peer Hello shows different HelloInt or DeadInt than local interface config confirms interval mismatch
verify_commands.look_for: Hello packets from peer showing HelloInt and DeadInt fields; compare against local values

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf neighbor'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Missing neighbor entry with active OSPF on both sides and Hellos being sent confirms a parameter mismatch causing Hello rejection
verify_commands.look_for: Absence of expected neighbor or neighbor stuck in Down state

verify_commands.confirms_if: Log messages mentioning Hello Interval or Dead Interval mismatch confirm the cause
verify_commands.look_for: Interval mismatch log messages from ospfd

failure_modes.description: OSPF routers on the same physical segment have different Area IDs configured on their interfaces. OSPF Hello packets contain the Area ID in their IP header (technically, in the OSPF common header). If the Area ID in a received Hello does not match the receiving interface's configured Area ID, the packet is silently discarded. The neighbor never appears and the adjacency never starts. Unlike Hello/Dead interval mismatch, there is usually no log message for Area ID mismatch in default FRR logging level.

resolutions.action: Enable OSPF packet debugging temporarily: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "debug ospf packet hello recv"'
Check logs for area mismatch: 'docker logs bgp 2>&1 | grep -i "area" | tail -20'
Verify area assignment on interface: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf interface <ifname>"' -- shows 'Area <id>'
Confirm peer's area assignment via out-of-band or by reading their Hello packet (area field is in OSPF common header)
Correct the area assignment: 'interface <ifname>' then 'ip ospf area <correct-area>' in vtysh
Disable debug after confirmation: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "no debug ospf packet hello recv"'

root_causes.cause: Interface on one router configured in area 0.0.0.0 (backbone) and the other in area 0.0.0.1 (or any non-matching area)
Mistyped area ID in the 'network <prefix> area <id>' command under 'router ospf'
Area ID entered as dotted-decimal on one side and as integer on the other (area 1 vs area 0.0.0.1 -- these are equivalent in FRR, but area 0.0.0.10 is different from area 10)
Interface-level 'ip ospf area <id>' overrides the router-level 'network' statement and was configured incorrectly

failure_modes.scenario: Area ID mismatch -- neighbors ignore each other's Hellos
symptoms: No OSPF neighbor relationship forms despite both routers having OSPF enabled on the interface
'show ip ospf neighbor' shows no entry for the peer
'show ip ospf interface' shows the interface as 'OSPF is enabled' with correct network statement
tcpdump confirms Hellos are arriving from the peer
ospfd debug logs (debug ospf packet hello recv) show 'area id mismatch'

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf interface <ifname>'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Area ID differs from what is configured on the peer's interface confirms area mismatch
verify_commands.look_for: Area field -- shows which OSPF area this interface belongs to

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Expected area ID absent from the OSPF process confirms the interface was not assigned to the correct area
verify_commands.look_for: All areas listed under 'Number of areas in this router'; verify the correct area exists

verify_commands.confirms_if: Peer's Hello shows different Area ID than local interface's configured area confirms mismatch
verify_commands.look_for: Area ID field in OSPF common header of peer's Hello packets

failure_modes.description: OSPF adjacency reaches Full state successfully (Hello/Dead intervals and Area IDs match), but routes from external sources or from other areas are missing. This happens when routers on the same area have inconsistent area type configuration: one router treats the area as a stub and another as a normal area, or one treats it as NSSA and another as stub. The adjacency itself forms because area type is not checked during Hello exchange on some FRR versions, but external LSAs (Type-5) are dropped by the stub router, causing route black-holes.

resolutions.action: Check area type on all routers in the area: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf"' -- look for area type in output
Check OSPF database for LSA types: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf database"' -- stub areas should have no Type-5 LSAs
Align area type configuration on all ABRs and internal routers for the area
For NSSA: ensure ABR has 'area <id> nssa' and internal routers also have 'area <id> nssa'
Restart ospfd after area type change: 'docker exec bgp supervisorctl restart ospfd' (disrupts all OSPF adjacencies briefly)

root_causes.cause: One router has 'area <id> stub' and another has no stub configuration (normal area) -- stub router drops Type-5 LSAs
One router has 'area <id> stub no-summary' (totally stubby) and another has plain 'area <id> stub' -- inter-area routes missing
One router has 'area <id> nssa' and another has 'area <id> stub' -- incompatible area type flags in Hello N-bit/E-bit
ABR configured as NSSA but internal routers not configured as NSSA -- Type-7 LSAs not generated or translated

failure_modes.scenario: Area type mismatch -- adjacency forms but routes not exchanged correctly
symptoms: OSPF adjacency shows Full state for the neighbor
Routes from redistributed sources (BGP, static, connected) are missing in the stub area
Type-5 (AS External) or Type-7 (NSSA External) LSAs absent from 'show ip ospf database' on stub/NSSA routers
Routers in stub area have only a default route but no external routes
ospfd logs may show LSA type filtering messages

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Different area types on routers in the same area confirms the mismatch causing LSA filtering
verify_commands.look_for: Area description -- 'Stub', 'NSSA', or normal; all routers in area must show same type

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf database'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Type-5 LSAs present on a router configured as stub confirms area type mismatch with the ABR
verify_commands.look_for: Presence or absence of 'AS External Link States' section; stub areas must not have Type-5 LSAs

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf database summary'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Missing LSA types that should be present (or unexpected LSAs that should be filtered) confirms area type inconsistency
verify_commands.look_for: LSA counts by type per area; Type-3 Summary LSAs absent in totally-stubby areas

failure_modes.description: Two OSPF neighbors successfully exchange Hello packets (reaching 2-Way) and begin database exchange, but the adjacency never advances past ExStart or Exchange state. The MTU field in Database Description (DBD) packets defaults to the interface MTU. If one side's interface MTU is smaller than the other's DBD packet, the DBD is silently dropped. The sending side retransmits indefinitely; the receiving side discards packets larger than its MTU. This is the second most common OSPF failure mode in SONiC environments where interface MTU may differ between different NIC types or configurations.

resolutions.action: Check local interface MTU: 'ip link show <interface>' -- look for mtu value
Check peer interface MTU out-of-band or via LLDP: 'show lldp neighbors detail | grep -A5 <port>'
Option 1 (preferred): Align MTUs on both interfaces to the same value
Option 2 (workaround): Configure 'ip ospf mtu-ignore' on the interface in vtysh to skip MTU check in DBD: 'interface <ifname>' then 'ip ospf mtu-ignore'
Verify neighbor advances: after MTU fix, 'show ip ospf neighbor' should reach Full within a few seconds

root_causes.cause: Interface MTU configured differently on the two ends of the link (common in SONiC with jumbo frames on some ports)
One interface has jumbo MTU (9100 or 9000) and the other has default Ethernet MTU (1500)
MTU mismatch introduced by VLAN tagging adding overhead not accounted for in OSPF MTU field
VxLAN or GRE tunnel overhead reduces effective MTU but OSPF does not account for the encapsulation
Hardware asymmetry: two different NIC types with different default MTUs in a SONiC chassis

failure_modes.scenario: MTU mismatch -- adjacency stuck in ExStart or Exchange
symptoms: 'show ip ospf neighbor' shows ExStart or Exchange state that never advances to Loading or Full
'show ip ospf neighbor detail' shows increasing 'Retransmit' count and zero 'Database Summary List' progress
Neighbor has been in ExStart/Exchange for minutes without advancing
tcpdump shows DBD packets being sent from one side but no corresponding DBD ACKs from the other
Interface MTU values differ between the two connected interfaces

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf neighbor detail'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Neighbor stuck in ExStart/Exchange with growing retransmit count confirms DBD exchange failure
verify_commands.look_for: State is ExStart or Exchange; high Retransmit count; Database Summary List length not decreasing

verify_commands.confirms_if: MTU values differ between the two ends of the link confirms the root cause
verify_commands.look_for: mtu value -- compare local MTU against peer's interface MTU

verify_commands.confirms_if: One-way DBD traffic with no acknowledgment confirms DBD packets are being silently dropped by the peer
verify_commands.look_for: DBD packets being sent but no DBD ACK in return; retransmitted DBD sequences (same DD sequence number resent)

verify_commands.confirms_if: LLDP-advertised MTU differs from local MTU confirms the link has asymmetric MTU configuration
verify_commands.look_for: Peer's port MTU (if advertised via LLDP); compare against local interface MTU

failure_modes.description: OSPF authentication (MD5 or plain-text) is configured on one side of a link but not the other, or both sides have authentication enabled but with different keys. OSPF treats authentication failures as silent drops -- no error is sent to the peer. The router discards the Hello without processing it, so the neighbor never advances past Down state. This is extremely difficult to diagnose without packet capture because ospfd does not log authentication failures at default log levels.

resolutions.action: Enable OSPF authentication debugging temporarily: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "debug ospf packet all recv"'
Check logs for authentication failure: 'docker logs bgp 2>&1 | grep -i "auth\|password\|key" | tail -20'
Verify authentication config on interface: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf interface <ifname>"' -- shows authentication type
Check FRR running-config for key strings: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show running-config"' | grep -A3 'ip ospf authentication'
Align authentication type and key on both sides; use MD5 with matching key-id and key string
After aligning, disable debug: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "no debug ospf packet all recv"'

root_causes.cause: Authentication enabled on one interface but not the other (type mismatch: null vs MD5 or plain-text)
Correct authentication type on both sides but different MD5 key-id or key string
Key rolled over on one side during a maintenance window but not the other
Plain-text password changed on one side and not replicated
frrcfgd applied authentication config from CONFIG_DB to wrong interface
Authentication key configured via direct vtysh on one side and via frrcfgd on the other, causing divergence

failure_modes.scenario: Authentication mismatch -- Hellos rejected silently
symptoms: No OSPF neighbor forms despite correct Hello/Dead intervals and area configuration
tcpdump shows Hellos arriving from peer but neighbor stays in Down state
'show ip ospf interface' shows authentication type configured but no neighbors
ospfd debug authentication logs show 'authentication failed' messages
Hellos are being sent out but no 2-Way or higher state is reached

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf interface <ifname>'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Authentication type differs from expected or from peer configuration confirms auth type mismatch
verify_commands.look_for: Authentication type field: None, Simple Password, or Cryptographic; key-id if MD5

verify_commands.confirms_if: Different Auth Type values in sent and received Hello packets confirms authentication mismatch
verify_commands.look_for: Auth Type field in OSPF Hello header: 0=None, 1=Simple, 2=MD5; compare sent vs received

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show running-config' | grep -A5 'ip ospf authentication'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Different authentication settings than the peer confirms the mismatch; absence of authentication when peer has it confirms type mismatch
verify_commands.look_for: Authentication type and key-id configured on the interface

failure_modes.description: On multi-access broadcast networks (Ethernet), OSPF elects a Designated Router (DR) and Backup Designated Router (BDR) to reduce the number of full adjacencies required. All routers form Full adjacency only with the DR and BDR. If the DR/BDR election produces unexpected results (wrong router elected, no DR, or priority misconfigured), non-DR routers may not form full adjacencies with each other and routing may be suboptimal or broken. In SONiC deployments, multi-access OSPF is common on management VLANs and server-facing segments.

resolutions.action: Check current DR/BDR and priorities: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf neighbor detail"' and 'show ip ospf interface <ifname>'
Set router priority: 'interface <ifname>' then 'ip ospf priority <0-255>' (higher = more likely to be DR; 0 = ineligible)
Check for duplicate Router IDs: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf database router"' -- each router should have unique advertising Router ID
To force new DR election on a segment, clear OSPF process: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "clear ip ospf process"' (disrupts all adjacencies)
For spine-leaf fabrics, consider using point-to-point network type to avoid DR/BDR entirely: 'ip ospf network point-to-point' on the interface

root_causes.cause: OSPF router priority not configured -- all routers default to priority 1 and the router with highest Router ID wins (may not be the desired router)
Priority set to 0 on the intended DR making it ineligible for election
DR non-preemption: once a DR is elected, it stays DR even if a higher-priority router joins; router must be restarted to trigger new election
Multiple routers on same segment have duplicate Router IDs -- election produces inconsistent results
Partially connected broadcast segment (not all routers can hear each other) causing split DR/BDR views

failure_modes.scenario: DR/BDR election issues on broadcast networks -- suboptimal or failed election
symptoms: 'show ip ospf neighbor' shows some neighbors in 2-Way instead of Full (DROther-to-DROther, expected)
Wrong router is acting as DR (not the intended one with highest priority)
Adjacency count is lower than expected -- some routers not reaching Full with DR/BDR
DR or BDR role changes frequently, causing SPF recalculations
'show ip ospf interface' shows router as DROther when it should be DR

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf interface <ifname>'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Unexpected DR IP or wrong role for local router confirms DR/BDR election issue
verify_commands.look_for: DR and BDR IP addresses, local router's role (DR/BDR/DROther), router priority

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf neighbor'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Fewer Full adjacencies than expected DR/BDR count confirms election problem or adjacency formation failure with DR
verify_commands.look_for: Neighbors in 2-Way state (DROther to DROther is normal); any Full adjacency should be with DR or BDR only on broadcast segments

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Duplicate Router IDs across routers in the area confirms the election will be unpredictable
verify_commands.look_for: Router ID -- check for duplicate Router IDs across all OSPF routers in the area

failure_modes.description: The OSPF LSDB is being flooded with rapidly changing LSAs, causing ospfd to run SPF (Dijkstra) repeatedly and consuming high CPU. This happens when a link or router is unstable (flapping), causing it to repeatedly originate and withdraw its Router-LSA. Each LSA change triggers a new SPF run. In SONiC, a high ospfd CPU load can also impact other processes in the bgp container including zebra and fpmsyncd, potentially causing OSPF-adjacent issues upstream.

resolutions.action: Identify the flapping LSA source: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf database"' -- look for LSAs with rapidly incrementing LS Sequence Numbers
Find the unstable router: the Advertising Router field of the rapidly-changing LSA identifies the source
Check that router's interface stability: 'show interfaces status' or check syslog for link-down events
Configure SPF throttling if not set: 'timers throttle spf 200 1000 10000' under 'router ospf'
For redistribution storms: add route-map filters to OSPF redistribution to suppress unstable prefixes
Check ospfd container stability: 'docker inspect bgp | grep RestartCount' -- ospfd crash-restart generates new LSA sequence numbers

root_causes.cause: A link or interface is flapping, causing the connected router to repeatedly originate new Router-LSAs
ospfd process on a neighbor is oscillating (crash-restart cycle generating new self-originate LSAs each time)
LSA maxage flushing storm: a router is originating many LSAs and the LSRefreshTime is close to a network change cycle
Route redistribution instability: external routes being redistributed into OSPF are flapping (e.g., BGP prefixes flapping cause OSPF Type-5 LSA churn)
ospfd SPF throttle timers not configured, causing immediate SPF on every LSA arrival

failure_modes.scenario: LSA flooding storm -- excessive SPF recalculations and high CPU
symptoms: ospfd or bgp container CPU usage is high: 'docker stats bgp --no-stream' shows elevated CPU
'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf spf-calculation"' shows high SPF run count or rapid last-run timestamps
Frequent log messages: 'SPF: Scheduled' or 'OSPF-5-ADJCHG' in syslog
Routing table instability -- routes flapping in and out
High OSPF packet rate visible in interface counters

verify_commands.confirms_if: High bgp container CPU during OSPF convergence events confirms excessive SPF recalculation
verify_commands.look_for: CPU % for the bgp container -- ospfd flooding storm typically pushes CPU above 50-80%

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf database'
verify_commands.confirms_if: High sequence numbers (0x80000100+ means 256+ originations) on a specific router's LSA identifies the flapping source
verify_commands.look_for: LSA Sequence Numbers -- rapidly incrementing sequence numbers for the same LSA indicate the originating router is repeatedly re-originating it

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf spf-calculation'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Very high SPF run count or frequent last-run timestamps confirms excessive SPF recalculation
verify_commands.look_for: SPF run count and timestamp of last SPF; multiple SPFs per minute indicates a flooding storm

verify_commands.confirms_if: Repeated SPF and adjacency events within seconds of each other confirms routing instability driven by LSA flooding
verify_commands.look_for: Rapid repeated SPF schedule messages or adjacency change events

failure_modes.description: OSPF and BGP are both running and routes are being redistributed in both directions -- OSPF routes into BGP and BGP routes into OSPF. Without proper filtering, a route learned from BGP gets redistributed into OSPF as an external route, then re-learned by BGP as an OSPF-sourced route, then redistributed back into OSPF with a different metric, creating a churn loop. Each redistribution cycle increments the OSPF LS Sequence Number on the external LSA, driving an LSA flooding storm and potentially causing route metric inflation.

resolutions.action: Add OSPF route tagging to prevent re-import: use a route-map to tag BGP routes when redistributing into OSPF, then deny tagged routes when redistributing OSPF back into BGP
Example: in 'redistribute bgp' into OSPF, add 'route-map tag-bgp-routes' that sets tag 100; in BGP's 'redistribute ospf', add 'route-map deny-tag-100' that denies routes with tag 100
Use prefix-lists to explicitly define which prefixes should be redistributed in each direction
Check current redistribution config: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show running-config"' -- look for 'redistribute' lines under both 'router ospf' and 'router bgp'
Remove bidirectional redistribution if not needed and replace with manual network statements

root_causes.cause: Mutual redistribution configured: 'redistribute bgp' under 'router ospf' AND 'redistribute ospf' under 'router bgp' without filtering
Missing route-map to tag redistributed routes and filter tagged routes on re-import
Incorrect administrative distance allowing OSPF external routes to win over directly learned BGP routes
Multiple redistribution points (more than one ABR or ASBR redistributing the same prefixes)

failure_modes.scenario: Route redistribution loop -- mutual redistribution between OSPF and BGP
symptoms: OSPF Type-5 or Type-7 external LSA database is very large and growing
Routes appear with unexpected OSPF external metrics or oscillating metrics
BGP and OSPF RIBs show routes with alternating source protocols
ospfd CPU is high due to frequent LSA re-origination
Routes that should be BGP-only appear in 'show ip ospf database external' with OSPF External type

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf database external'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Large and growing external LSA database with BGP-originated routes confirms redistribution loop
verify_commands.look_for: Large number of Type-5 LSAs; LSAs with rapidly incrementing sequence numbers; routes that should be internal appearing as external

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show running-config' | grep -A2 redistribute
verify_commands.confirms_if: Mutual redistribution without filtering tags confirms the loop configuration exists
verify_commands.look_for: Presence of 'redistribute bgp' under 'router ospf' AND 'redistribute ospf' under 'router bgp' without route-maps

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf database external' | grep -c 'Link State ID'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Unexpectedly large external LSA count confirms route redistribution is generating excessive entries
verify_commands.look_for: Count of external LSAs -- should be small (tens to low hundreds in a normal network); thousands indicates redistribution explosion

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip route ospf'
verify_commands.confirms_if: BGP-originated prefixes showing as OSPF External routes on the redistributing router confirms the loop
verify_commands.look_for: OSPF external (O E1/E2) routes that correspond to BGP prefixes -- if BGP prefixes appear as OSPF external routes on the local router, the loop is active

failure_modes.description: OSPF adjacency is Full and OSPF routes appear in 'show ip route ospf' in the FRR/zebra RIB, but traffic to OSPF-learned destinations is dropped. The routes have been installed into the Linux kernel routing table by zebra but fpmsyncd has not published them to APPL_DB ROUTE_TABLE, so orchagent never programs them into the ASIC. This is the same pipeline failure as with BGP routes but manifests in OSPF deployments. In SONiC, all routes (regardless of protocol) must flow through fpmsyncd to reach the ASIC.

resolutions.action: Check fpmsyncd status: 'docker exec bgp supervisorctl status fpmsyncd'
Check FPM connection: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show zebra fpm stats"' -- should show 'connected' and message counts
Check fpmsyncd logs: 'docker logs bgp 2>&1 | grep -i fpmsyncd | tail -30'
Restart fpmsyncd (safe, does not affect OSPF adjacencies): 'docker exec bgp supervisorctl restart fpmsyncd'
After restart, verify APPL_DB is populated: 'redis-cli -n 0 KEYS "ROUTE_TABLE:*" | wc -l'
If APPL_DB shows routes but ASIC_DB does not, the failure is in orchagent: 'docker logs swss 2>&1 | grep -E "ERROR|route" | tail -30'

root_causes.cause: fpmsyncd process has crashed or stalled inside the bgp container
FPM socket between zebra and fpmsyncd is broken -- zebra has installed routes in kernel but FPM notification was not sent
fpmsyncd Redis connection to APPL_DB is broken
zebra FPM module not loaded or misconfigured -- check 'show zebra fpm stats'
fpmsyncd restart without full kernel route resync -- routes added to kernel before fpmsyncd restarted are not re-pushed

failure_modes.scenario: OSPF routes not reaching ASIC -- fpmsyncd not syncing OSPF routes from zebra
symptoms: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip route ospf"' shows OSPF routes in zebra RIB
Traffic to OSPF-learned prefixes is dropped or sent to wrong next-hop
APPL_DB ROUTE_TABLE is missing OSPF-learned prefixes: 'redis-cli -n 0 KEYS "ROUTE_TABLE:*"' shows incomplete table
ASIC_DB has no route entry for the OSPF prefix: 'redis-cli -n 1 KEYS "ASIC_STATE:SAI_OBJECT_TYPE_ROUTE_ENTRY*" | grep <prefix>'
fpmsyncd logs show errors or process is in failed state

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip route ospf'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Routes present in zebra RIB but absent in APPL_DB confirms fpmsyncd pipeline failure
verify_commands.look_for: OSPF routes (marked 'O' for intra-area, 'O IA' for inter-area, 'O E1'/'O E2' for external) in zebra RIB

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp supervisorctl status fpmsyncd
verify_commands.confirms_if: STOPPED or FATAL state confirms fpmsyncd is not publishing OSPF routes to APPL_DB
verify_commands.look_for: fpmsyncd process state: RUNNING expected

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show zebra fpm stats'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Disconnected state or zero message count confirms zebra's FPM socket to fpmsyncd is broken
verify_commands.look_for: FPM connection status (connected/disconnected), messages sent/received count

verify_commands.confirms_if: Count significantly lower than number of routes in zebra RIB confirms fpmsyncd is not publishing routes
verify_commands.look_for: Count of routes in APPL_DB -- should include OSPF routes visible in zebra RIB

verify_commands.confirms_if: Key absent in ASIC_DB while present in APPL_DB confirms orchagent or syncd failure; key absent in both confirms fpmsyncd failure
verify_commands.look_for: Matching key for the OSPF route prefix in ASIC_DB

failure_modes.description: OSPF requires all non-backbone areas (area != 0.0.0.0) to connect directly to the backbone area (area 0). When an area's only connection to the backbone is via a transit area, a virtual link through that transit area must be configured. If the virtual link fails (transit area loses connectivity, virtual link neighbor goes down, or virtual link is misconfigured), the attached area loses backbone connectivity and loses all inter-area routes. This manifests as the virtual link neighbor disappearing from 'show ip ospf virtual-links'.

resolutions.action: Check virtual link state: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf virtual-links"' -- should show 'Full' state
Verify transit area reachability: check that a path exists through the transit area to the remote ABR
Verify transit area is not stub/NSSA: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf"' -- transit area must be a normal area
Check virtual link configuration matches on both ABRs: same transit area ID and correct remote router IDs
Verify remote ABR Router ID: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "show ip ospf"' on the remote ABR -- Router ID must match what is configured in 'area <transit> virtual-link <router-id>'
Clear virtual link to force re-establishment: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c "clear ip ospf process"' on the ABR (disrupts all adjacencies)

root_causes.cause: Transit area lost its connection to one or both virtual link endpoints
Virtual link endpoint router ID changed (ospfd restart with different Router ID) and virtual link config not updated
Authentication mismatch on the virtual link
Hello/Dead interval mismatch specifically for the virtual link interface
Transit area configured as stub or NSSA -- virtual links cannot traverse stub or NSSA areas (RFC 2328 requirement)
ospfd on the ABR restarted and virtual link state was not re-established

failure_modes.scenario: Virtual link failure -- backbone continuity broken
symptoms: 'show ip ospf virtual-links' shows virtual link neighbor in Down state or absent
Routers in the non-backbone area lose all inter-area (O IA) routes from 'show ip route'
Only intra-area routes remain in the disconnected area
ospfd logs show virtual link adjacency down events
Connectivity from the isolated area to other areas and external routes is lost

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf virtual-links'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Virtual link in Down state confirms backbone connectivity is broken for the attached non-backbone area
verify_commands.look_for: Virtual link state: Full is operational; Down means the virtual link has failed

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip ospf'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Transit area shown as stub or NSSA confirms the virtual link cannot function through it per RFC 2328
verify_commands.look_for: Virtual link neighbor count; transit area type (must be normal, not stub/NSSA)

verify_commands.command: docker exec bgp vtysh -c 'show ip route ospf'
verify_commands.confirms_if: Missing inter-area routes confirms the area has lost backbone connectivity via the virtual link
verify_commands.look_for: Absence of 'O IA' (inter-area) routes in the isolated area's routing table

verify_commands.confirms_if: Virtual link down log messages confirm the virtual link failure event and timing
verify_commands.look_for: Virtual link adjacency down or configuration error messages from ospfd

messages.description: Discovers neighbors, negotiates parameters, elects DR/BDR
messages.name: Hello

messages.description: Exchanges LSDB summary during adjacency formation
messages.name: Database Description (DBD)
Link State Request (LSR)
messages.when_sent: After DBD exchange when missing LSAs are identified

messages.description: Carries one or more LSAs — the core flooding mechanism
messages.name: Link State Update (LSU)
messages.when_sent: In response to LSR, or when LSA originates/changes

messages.description: Used during the ExStart and Exchange states to synchronize LSDBs. Contains LSA headers (without full LSA data) summarizing the sender's LSDB. The master sends DBDs with incrementing sequence numbers; the slave echoes each sequence number to acknowledge. The M (More) bit indicates more DBD packets to follow.
key_fields: Interface MTU: must not exceed neighbor's interface MTU or DBD is rejected (key field causing ExStart stuck)
DD Sequence Number: monotonically increasing; used for reliable exchange
LSA Headers: Type-1 through Type-5/7 LSA headers describing LSDB contents
messages.name: DBD (Database Description)
messages.when_sent: During ExStart (to negotiate master/slave) and Exchange (to describe LSDB contents)

messages.description: Sent during Loading state to request specific LSAs from the neighbor that were identified as missing or newer during the DBD exchange. A router sends LSRs for every LSA it needs; the neighbor responds with LSU packets containing the full LSA data.
key_fields: LS Type: type of requested LSA (1=Router, 2=Network, 3=Summary, 4=ASBR Summary, 5=AS External, 7=NSSA External)
Link State ID: identifies the LSA within its type
Advertising Router: Router ID of the LSA's originator
messages.name: LSR (Link State Request)
messages.when_sent: During Loading state after DBD exchange identifies LSAs needed from the neighbor

messages.description: Carries one or more complete LSAs in response to LSR packets, or as part of link-state flooding when a topology change occurs. LSUs are sent as unicast in response to LSRs, and multicast (224.0.0.5 or 224.0.0.6) during flooding. The receiving router must send an LSAck.
key_fields: Number of LSAs: count of LSAs in this packet
LSAs: one or more complete LSAs (Router-LSA, Network-LSA, Summary-LSA, AS External-LSA, etc.)
LSA Header: LS Age, Options, LS Type, Link State ID, Advertising Router, LS Sequence Number, LS Checksum, Length
messages.name: LSU (Link State Update)
messages.when_sent: In response to LSR during Loading state; during flooding when a new or updated LSA is received or originated locally

messages.description: Acknowledges receipt of LSU packets. OSPF uses reliable flooding -- every LSU must be acknowledged or the sender retransmits. LSAcks can be immediate (unicast) or delayed (multicast, batched). Missing LSAcks cause retransmission floods. High retransmission counts visible in 'show ip ospf neighbor detail' indicate LSAck loss.
key_fields: LSA Headers: list of LSA headers being acknowledged (same format as LSA header in LSU)
messages.name: LSAck (Link State Acknowledgment)
messages.when_sent: After receiving LSU packets during flooding or Loading state; can be delayed to batch multiple ACKs

purpose: Open Shortest Path First version 2 — link-state IGP for IPv4 routing

states.description: No Hello received from neighbor. Initial state for all neighbors. On NBMA networks, Hellos may be sent to this neighbor at a reduced rate (PollInterval).
states.name: DOWN

states.description: (NBMA only) Unicast Hello sent to a manually configured neighbor, but no Hello has been received in reply yet. This state applies only to NBMA and Point-to-Multipoint non-broadcast network types.
states.name: ATTEMPT

states.description: Hello received from neighbor but own Router ID is not listed in the neighbor's Hello packet. This means communication is one-way. Stuck in INIT indicates a one-way communication problem — return Hellos are not reaching the neighbor.
states.name: INIT

states.description: Bidirectional communication established — own Router ID has been seen in the neighbor's Hello. DR/BDR election occurs in this state on broadcast and NBMA networks. This is the steady state for DROther-to-DROther adjacencies on multi-access segments (no full adjacency needed).
states.name: 2-WAY

states.description: Master/slave negotiation for Database Description (DBD) exchange. The router with the higher Router ID becomes master. Sequence numbers are synchronized. Stuck in EXSTART almost always indicates MTU mismatch or authentication failure.
states.name: EXSTART

states.description: Database Description packets are being exchanged. Each DBD contains LSA headers summarizing the sender's LSDB. The receiver builds a list of LSAs it needs to request.
states.name: EXCHANGE

states.description: Link State Requests (LSRs) have been sent for LSAs identified during EXCHANGE. Waiting for Link State Updates (LSUs) in response.
states.name: LOADING

states.description: Fully adjacent — LSDBs are synchronized between neighbors. This is the normal steady state for DR/BDR adjacencies, point-to-point links, and virtual links. Routes are computed from the synchronized LSDB.
states.name: FULL

timers.name: hello-interval

os_defaults.config_path: Not in CONFIG_DB; configured via frrcfgd or vtysh: 'ip ospf hello-interval <seconds>' on interface

timers.tuning_advice: Must match all neighbors on a segment; lower for faster detection but more overhead

timers.description: Time without a Hello before declaring neighbor down
timers.name: dead-interval
timers.on_expiry: Neighbor declared dead; adjacency torn down; routes removed

os_defaults.config_path: Not in CONFIG_DB; configured via frrcfgd or vtysh: 'ip ospf dead-interval <seconds>' on interface

timers.tuning_advice: Must match all neighbors; consider BFD for sub-second detection instead

timers.description: Time to wait before DR/BDR election on broadcast/NBMA
timers.name: wait-timer
LSA refresh
timers.on_expiry: LSA refreshed with incremented sequence number

timers.description: Maximum age before LSA is flushed
timers.name: LSA max-age
timers.on_expiry: LSA purged from LSDB and flooded with max-age

timers.description: Delay before running SPF calculation after a topology change
timers.name: SPF delay

os_defaults.config_path: Not in CONFIG_DB; configured via frrcfgd or vtysh: 'timers throttle spf <delay> <initial-hold> <max-hold>' under 'router ospf'
os_defaults.default: FRR ospfd default exponential backoff timers

timers.tuning_advice: Tune SPF timers (initial-delay, hold-time, max-wait) for faster convergence: e.g., 1ms/5ms/5000ms

timers.name: LSA throttle
timers.tuning_advice: Tune for faster convergence in large networks

timers.description: Every LSA carries an age field that starts at 0 and increments by 1 per second as it propagates and ages in the LSDB. When LSA age reaches MaxAge (3600s = 1 hour), the LSA is flushed from the LSDB and re-flooded as a MaxAge LSA. LSAs are refreshed by the originating router every LSRefreshTime (30 minutes) before they reach MaxAge.
timers.name: LSA Age / MaxAge
timers.on_expiry: LSA with age=MaxAge is re-flooded to signal deletion; all routers remove it from LSDB and re-run SPF

os_defaults.config_path: Not in CONFIG_DB; FRR defaults apply; not typically changed in SONiC deployments
os_defaults.default: MaxAge=3600s, LSRefreshTime=1800s (FRR ospfd defaults; RFC 2328 standard values)

timers.tuning_advice: Do not change MaxAge or LSRefreshTime in production. An LSDB flooded with MaxAge LSAs indicates an unstable topology or a router repeatedly originating and flushing LSAs.

transitions.trigger: Hello received from neighbor
Own Router ID seen in neighbor's Hello (bidirectional)
Adjacency decision — at least one side is DR/BDR, or point-to-point link
Master/slave agreed, sequence numbers synchronized
All DBDs exchanged, LSRs needed
All DBDs exchanged, no LSRs needed (databases already in sync)
All LSAs received and acknowledged
Dead timer expired — no Hellos received
Dead timer expired

transitions.failure_if_stuck: MTU mismatch or authentication failure preventing DBD exchange
transitions.trigger: Dead timer expired

transitions.failure_if_stuck: One-way communication — ACL, NAT, or asymmetric routing blocking return Hellos
transitions.trigger: Dead timer expired
Received Hello without own Router ID (neighbor restarted or misconfiguration)
(NBMA only) Neighbor manually configured, unicast Hello sent
Hello received from NBMA neighbor
Hello packet received from neighbor; the neighbor's Router ID is noted but our Router ID is not yet in their Hello neighbor list
NBMA network only: Hello sent to configured neighbor IP, waiting for response

transitions.failure_if_stuck: Asymmetric link: our Hellos reach the neighbor but their Hellos do not reach us (or vice versa). Check ACLs, multicast group join (224.0.0.5), interface MTU mismatch for Hello packets, or OSPF disabled on the neighbor's interface.
transitions.trigger: Hello received with local Router ID in the neighbor list -- bidirectional communication confirmed
DR/BDR election complete (broadcast networks) or adjacency decision made (point-to-point); master/slave negotiation begins

transitions.failure_if_stuck: MTU mismatch: one side drops DBD packets that exceed its interface MTU. Check 'ip ospf mtu-ignore' and interface MTU on both sides. Also check authentication mismatch causing DBD rejection.
transitions.trigger: Master/slave relationship established via DBD with I/M/MS bits; initial sequence number agreed upon

transitions.failure_if_stuck: MTU mismatch causing large DBD packets to be dropped. Authentication issues. Check 'show ip ospf neighbor detail' for retransmission counts.
transitions.trigger: All DBD packets exchanged; routers have complete lists of LSA headers; LSR packets sent for missing LSAs

transitions.failure_if_stuck: LSR/LSU packet loss; corrupted LSA causing repeated request-response cycle; neighbor does not originate the requested LSA. Check retransmit queue: 'show ip ospf neighbor detail'.
transitions.trigger: All requested LSAs received via LSU; LSDB is fully synchronized
Dead Interval expires (no Hello received within dead-interval seconds), interface goes down, or 'clear ip ospf' issued
On broadcast network: DR or BDR failure causes DROther to lose Full adjacency with DR/BDR; 2-Way maintained with other DROthers
Dead Interval expires or interface goes down

notes.note: OSPF available without feature enable. Supports both network statements and 'ip ospf <process> area <area>' under interface. Reference bandwidth default is 100 Mbps. NSF/NSR supported.

TRAP: 'router ospf1' (no space before process number) and 'router ospf 1' (with space) create DIFFERENT OSPF processes in IOS-XE. The typo 'router ospf1' creates a process literally named 'ospf1', which silently succeeds but runs as a separate process from the intended 'ospf 1'. Always verify process names with 'show ip ospf'.

IOS-XE interface-level 'ip ospf <process> area <area>' overrides the global 'network' statement. If both are configured for the same interface, the interface-level command takes precedence. This can cause confusion when the interface appears in a different area than the network statement suggests.

OSPF marks Hello packets with DSCP CS6 (48) by default. If transit infrastructure QoS policies remark or deprioritize CS6, OSPF Hellos may be dropped under congestion, causing neighbor flapping. Verify DSCP preservation end-to-end when OSPF runs over QoS-managed infrastructure.

IOS-XE supports Embedded Packet Capture (EPC) for capturing OSPF packets at wire level: 'monitor capture <name> interface <intf> both' with ACL filtering for protocol 89. Complement with 'debug platform packet-trace' for FIA-level processing visibility. EPC exports to PCAP for Wireshark analysis.

IOS-XE punt policer may rate-limit OSPF packets on high-scale platforms. If many OSPF adjacencies are forming simultaneously (e.g., after reload), the control-plane punt policer may drop OSPF packets. Check with 'show platform software punt-policer' and verify OSPF queue is not being rate-limited.

OSPF configured under 'router ospf <process>' with area and interface hierarchy. No network statements — interfaces explicitly listed under area. Reference bandwidth default is 100 Mbps (should set to 'auto-cost reference-bandwidth 100000' for modern speeds). NSR supported.
IOS-XR OSPF supports segment routing natively under the OSPF process. Prefix-SID configured under interface.
OSPF requires 'feature ospf' to enable. Supports 'ip ospf bfd' for BFD integration. Reference bandwidth default is 40 Gbps. Graceful restart (NSF) supported.
NX-OS uses 'ip router ospf <process> area <area>' under interface instead of network statements under router ospf. Both methods work but interface-level is recommended.

relevant_fields: hostname: device hostname, used as reference for OSPF router identity
NULL: presence indicates interface is managed by SONiC
ip: IP address and prefix length assigned to interface
vrf_name: VRF assignment for the interface
NULL: presence indicates loopback is managed
ip: loopback IP used as OSPF router-id if it is the highest configured loopback
nexthop: comma-separated list of next-hop IPs
distance: administrative distance (OSPF intra=110, inter=110, external=150)

notes.note: OSPF in SONiC runs inside the 'bgp' Docker container via FRR (Free Range Routing), the same container as bgpd and zebra. ospfd handles the OSPF FSM, LSA database, and SPF computation. zebra receives OSPF routes and installs them into the Linux kernel RIB. The OSPF route programming pipeline (ospfd -> zebra -> fpmsyncd -> APPL_DB -> orchagent -> ASIC_DB -> syncd) is identical to the BGP route pipeline -- all routes converge at zebra and fpmsyncd.

SONiC's CONFIG_DB support for OSPF is less complete than for BGP. Many SONiC deployments configure OSPF directly in /etc/frr/frr.conf or via vtysh, rather than through CONFIG_DB + frrcfgd. Check whether frrcfgd has OSPF template support in your SONiC build. In builds without frrcfgd OSPF support, direct FRR config changes persist across ospfd restarts but may be overwritten by a full 'config reload'. Always verify with 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c show running-config'.
notes.source_ref: sonic-buildimage:src/sonic-frr/frrcfgd/ -- check for ospf template files

notes.note: OSPF and BGP share the same 'bgp' container and the same zebra process in SONiC. Routes from both protocols are stored in zebra's RIB and forwarded to fpmsyncd. Administrative distance determines which protocol's route wins when both have a route to the same prefix: OSPF intra-area=110, OSPF inter-area=110, OSPF external E1/E2=150, BGP eBGP=20, BGP iBGP=200. Be aware of these interactions when both protocols are running simultaneously.
notes.source_ref: FRR zebra administrative distance defaults and SONiC bgp container architecture

notes.note: OSPF hello and dead interval configuration in SONiC/FRR is done at the interface level, not globally. Every interface participating in OSPF can have independent timer values. This is both a feature (optimization per link type) and a trap -- engineers often configure timers globally expecting them to apply everywhere, but FRR requires explicit per-interface configuration. Always verify with 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c show ip ospf interface <ifname>' after configuring timers.

On SONiC data-center switches with point-to-point links between routers, configure 'ip ospf network point-to-point' on each routed interface. This disables DR/BDR election (which is unnecessary on point-to-point links) and speeds up adjacency formation from 2-Way directly to ExStart without waiting for DR/BDR election. This is a best practice for spine-leaf SONiC fabrics using OSPF as the underlay IGP.
notes.source_ref: RFC 2328 Section 9.1 and FRR ospfd network type configuration

notes.note: OSPF external route redistribution from BGP into OSPF ('redistribute bgp' under 'router ospf') should always include a route-map. Without a route-map, ALL BGP routes -- including BGP default routes and potentially thousands of prefixes -- are injected into the OSPF LSDB as Type-5 external LSAs. This can cause OSPF to flood thousands of LSAs to all routers in the OSPF domain and severely impact CPU and convergence time.
notes.source_ref: FRR ospfd redistribution and RFC 2328 AS External LSA flooding

notes.note: The OSPF Router ID in FRR is selected automatically from the highest configured IP address on any interface, preferring loopback interfaces. In SONiC, Loopback0 is the standard management loopback -- configure it first before enabling OSPF to ensure a stable Router ID. A Router ID change (due to loopback removal or ospfd restart without a configured loopback) causes ospfd to flush and re-originate all its LSAs, temporarily breaking all adjacencies.
notes.source_ref: FRR ospfd Router ID selection: ospfd/ospf_main.c

notes.note: BFD integration with OSPF in SONiC/FRR requires both ospfd and bfdd to be running. BFD for OSPF is configured per-interface ('ip ospf bfd') in vtysh. When BFD detects a link failure, it notifies ospfd which immediately tears down the OSPF adjacency -- much faster than waiting for the OSPF Dead Interval. Verify BFD sessions: 'docker exec bgp vtysh -c show bfd peers'. Note that BFD echo mode may not be supported on all SONiC ASIC platforms.
notes.source_ref: FRR bfdd and ospfd BFD integration: ospfd/ospf_bfd.c
